problems.name: Guitar String Buzz
problems.description: One or more guitar strings produce a buzzing sound when fretted or played open, indicating a setup or hardware issue.
visual_symptoms: String visibly vibrating against a fret during play
Uneven fret heights visible under string when holding bends
Nut slot visibly too deep for string gauge
Neck visibly bowed backward (back-bow) under string tension

probable_causes.cause: Insufficient neck relief causing strings to contact frets in the middle of the neck
probable_causes.test: Fret string at 1st and 14th fret simultaneously and check gap at 7th fret — less than 0.3 mm indicates back-bow

probable_causes.cause: Nut slot too low for the string gauge allowing string to buzz on first fret
probable_causes.test: Fret the string at the 3rd fret; open string should clear 1st fret by at least 0.2 mm

probable_causes.cause: High fret or uneven fret level causing localised buzz at specific positions
probable_causes.test: Use a precision straight edge across three consecutive frets to identify any fret higher than its neighbours

steps.instruction: Adjust truss rod by turning clockwise (tighten) in small 1/8-turn increments to add relief and correct back-bow
steps.verification: Gap at 7th fret with strings fretted at 1st and 14th measures 0.3–0.5 mm

steps.instruction: If buzz persists, raise saddle height at bridge by adjusting bridge saddle screws until string action is comfortable
steps.verification: String action at 12th fret measures approximately 2 mm bass side, 1.5 mm treble side

steps.instruction: If buzz is at open string only, build up nut slot with nut shim adhesive or replace the nut
steps.verification: Open string clears 1st fret by at least 0.3 mm; buzz eliminated

problems.name: Tuning Peg Slippage
problems.description: Tuning pegs slip out of tune during playing, failing to hold string tension consistently.
visual_symptoms: String visibly unwinding at peg after tuning
Tuning peg turns with no resistance when plucked
Peg post wobbles laterally when moved
Instrument falls out of tune within a few minutes of tuning

probable_causes.cause: Worn or loose tuner gear mechanism with insufficient friction
probable_causes.test: Tighten the small screw on the end of the tuner button; if gears still slip freely, gear is worn

probable_causes.cause: String wound incorrectly with too few or too many wraps causing slippage at post
probable_causes.test: Inspect number of string wraps on post — 2 to 3 winds are optimal for most tuners

probable_causes.cause: Nut slot binding and releasing string tension unpredictably
probable_causes.test: Apply graphite pencil to nut slot and observe if tuning stability improves immediately

steps.instruction: Tighten the tensioning screw on each tuner button with a small Phillips screwdriver until appropriate resistance is felt
steps.verification: Tuner button requires deliberate effort to turn; string pitch holds after stretching strings

steps.instruction: Restring with correct winding technique: 2–3 neat wraps below lock point, ensure string locks over itself
steps.verification: String pitch stabilises within 5 minutes of tuning and string stretching

steps.instruction: Apply graphite powder or dedicated nut lubricant to nut slots to eliminate binding
steps.verification: String glides smoothly through nut slot without catching; tuning remains stable across bends

problems.name: Pickup Noise and Hum
problems.description: Electric guitar or bass produces excessive hum, buzz or noise through the amplifier when not shielding the body.
visual_symptoms: Constant 50 or 60 Hz hum audible through amplifier
Noise reduces when player touches metal parts of guitar
Hum increases near computer monitors, fluorescent lights or transformers
Single-coil pickup hum absent on humbucking position

probable_causes.cause: Single-coil pickup picking up electromagnetic interference from nearby electronics
probable_causes.test: Move guitar away from monitors, transformers and power strips; hum reduction confirms EMI source

probable_causes.cause: Missing or broken ground connection between bridge, strings and output jack
probable_causes.test: Touch the bridge saddle — if hum disappears, the string ground wire is disconnected

probable_causes.cause: Unshielded pickup cavity allowing RFI ingress into signal path
probable_causes.test: Inspect pickup cavity for copper foil shielding; absence of shielding in cavity confirms cause

steps.instruction: Inspect and resolder the bridge ground wire to the back of the volume pot; ensure continuity from strings to output jack ground
steps.verification: Touching strings silences hum; continuity measured from string to sleeve of output jack

steps.instruction: Line the pickup and control cavities with copper tape shielding, connecting all foil sections to ground
steps.verification: Hum level reduced by at least 50% in problematic locations

steps.instruction: Reorient guitar body away from interference sources or switch to humbucking pickup position
steps.verification: Noise floor silent enough for clean recording at normal playing distance from monitor

problems.name: Digital Piano Key Unresponsive
problems.description: One or more keys on a digital piano or keyboard do not produce sound or produce sound at incorrect velocity.
visual_symptoms: Key depressed fully but no sound produced
Key produces sound only when pressed very hard
MIDI monitoring shows no note-on event for affected key
Key velocity consistently maximum or minimum regardless of touch

probable_causes.cause: Rubber contact dome under key worn flat, no longer making reliable contact with PCB
probable_causes.test: Remove key and inspect rubber dome for flat or cracked contact nub on underside

probable_causes.cause: Debris or dust on key contact PCB strip preventing electrical contact
probable_causes.test: Open keyboard and clean PCB contact strip with isopropyl alcohol; test key response

probable_causes.cause: Key mechanism hinge broken causing key to rest at incorrect angle missing contact point
probable_causes.test: Inspect key hinge at rear — broken plastic pivot will cause key to sit unevenly in rest position

steps.instruction: Remove the affected key by releasing its plastic hinge clip and lift out; clean the rubber dome contact with isopropyl alcohol
steps.verification: Contact dome nub clean and intact; key produces consistent response after reassembly

steps.instruction: If rubber dome is worn flat, replace the rubber dome sheet or individual dome for the affected key
steps.verification: New dome seated correctly; key velocity response now consistent across full press range

steps.instruction: Clean the corresponding PCB contact strip with isopropyl alcohol and lint-free cloth
steps.verification: PCB contacts visually clean and unoxidised; MIDI note-on events appear for all velocities

problems.name: Trumpet Valve Sticking
problems.description: One or more trumpet valves stick, move sluggishly or fail to return to up position after being pressed.
visual_symptoms: Valve requires extra force to depress fully
Valve returns slowly or not at all after being released
Valve makes grinding or rough sensation when operated
Partial depression causing air leak and airy tone

probable_causes.cause: Insufficient valve oil causing friction between valve and casing
probable_causes.test: Remove valve and check oil level on casing; dry or tacky feel confirms lack of lubrication

probable_causes.cause: Valve casing or valve body dented from impact, causing misalignment
probable_causes.test: Inspect casing exterior for dents; rotate valve 180 degrees and check if sticking position changes

probable_causes.cause: Residue buildup from old oil, mineral deposits or lacquer damage inside casing
probable_causes.test: Remove valve and inspect inside casing with torch for discolouration or residue on walls

steps.instruction: Remove the valve completely and wipe it and the inside of the casing clean with a lint-free cloth
steps.verification: Valve surface and casing walls clean with no old oil residue visible

steps.instruction: Apply 3–4 drops of quality valve oil to the valve body and reinsert, rotating to distribute oil evenly
steps.verification: Valve moves freely and returns to up position in under 0.5 seconds

steps.instruction: If sticking persists, soak valve in warm soapy water for 10 minutes and clean casing with brush; re-oil after drying
steps.verification: Valve operates freely throughout full stroke with consistent feel

problems.name: Clarinet Reed Crack
problems.description: Clarinet reed develops a crack or split, producing squeaks, thin tone or complete failure to sound.
visual_symptoms: Visible hairline crack along reed vamp or heart
Reed surface visually warped or curled when dry
Squeak or thin sound on specific notes
Reed chips at tip corner after brief use

probable_causes.cause: Reed dried out too quickly after playing causing stress crack from rapid moisture loss
probable_causes.test: Inspect crack origin — crack from tip indicates rapid drying; crack from side indicates clamping stress

probable_causes.cause: Ligature overtightened, cracking the reed at the ligature binding points
probable_causes.test: Check ligature screw tightness; cracks at ligature contact points confirm over-tightening

probable_causes.cause: Reed too soft for player's embouchure, causing collapse and splitting under bite pressure
probable_causes.test: Compare reed strength number with player's experience level — soft reeds (1–2) are more prone to cracking under firm embouchure

steps.instruction: Discard the cracked reed — cracked reeds cannot be reliably repaired and will produce inconsistent tone
steps.verification: Cracked reed removed from mouthpiece and disposed of

steps.instruction: Install a new reed of appropriate strength; soak for 30 seconds before use and break in over several sessions
steps.verification: New reed seals flat against mouthpiece table; full even tone across all registers

steps.instruction: Store reeds in a humidity-controlled reed case to prevent rapid drying between sessions
steps.verification: Reed remains flat and undistorted after 48 hours in case

problems.name: Drum Head Crack
problems.description: Drum head develops a crack, hole or wrinkling, causing degraded tone, buzzing or complete loss of resonance.
visual_symptoms: Visible crack or hole in drum head film
Wrinkled or puckered area on head surface
Buzzing or rattling overtone on every stroke
Dull thud instead of resonant drum sound

probable_causes.cause: Physical impact from hard drumstick or rim shot splitting the head film
probable_causes.test: Inspect head surface under direct light for cracks or punctures at impact zone

probable_causes.cause: Head over-tensioned beyond rated pitch causing stress failure
probable_causes.test: Compare tensioning key turns per lug with manufacturer's tuning guidelines for head diameter

probable_causes.cause: Old head material brittle from UV exposure or age, failing under normal playing
probable_causes.test: Check head age — heads over 2 years with regular use or showing yellowing should be considered for replacement

steps.instruction: Remove the cracked head by loosening all tension rods evenly in a star pattern and lifting off the hoop
steps.verification: Head removed cleanly; hoop and lug threads undamaged

steps.instruction: Install new head of matching diameter and type; seat hoop evenly and hand-tighten all tension rods before using key
steps.verification: Head seated evenly with no wrinkles before tensioning

steps.instruction: Tension head in a star pattern, one half-turn per rod, checking pitch by tapping near each lug until even
steps.verification: Pitch consistent at every lug position; desired fundamental tone achieved

problems.name: Metronome Sync Error
problems.description: Digital or mechanical metronome loses accurate tempo or shows inconsistent beat timing.
visual_symptoms: Metronome beat noticeably unsteady or drifting over time
LED or pendulum movement not matching audible click
Tempo readout shows different BPM from set value
Metronome stops mid-session without user interaction

probable_causes.cause: Low battery causing clock crystal to run below specification frequency
probable_causes.test: Check battery voltage — below 1.2V per cell for AA indicates insufficient supply for accurate oscillator

probable_causes.cause: Mechanical pendulum metronome with weight incorrectly positioned on rod
probable_causes.test: Verify weight position on pendulum rod matches BPM scale markings on housing

probable_causes.cause: Digital metronome firmware bug causing timer drift on certain BPM values
probable_causes.test: Compare metronome output against phone tuner metronome at same BPM; consistent offset indicates firmware issue

steps.instruction: Replace batteries with fresh alkaline cells; insert with correct polarity
steps.verification: Metronome powers on with steady display; tempo steady at reference BPM confirmed with phone metronome

steps.instruction: For mechanical metronome: reposition weight on rod to align with printed BPM marking and confirm tempo
steps.verification: Beat rate matches expected BPM counted over 60 seconds

problems.name: MIDI Connection Dropout
problems.description: MIDI signal between keyboard, controller or DAW drops out, causing missed notes or stuck notes.
visual_symptoms: Notes playing without release — stuck notes in DAW
MIDI activity LED not lighting for all played notes
Intermittent gaps in recorded MIDI in DAW session
DAW MIDI input monitor showing sporadic note events

probable_causes.cause: USB cable quality or length causing data errors in USB MIDI transfer
probable_causes.test: Replace USB cable with a certified USB 2.0 cable under 1.5 m and observe if dropouts reduce

probable_causes.cause: USB hub power insufficiency causing device to drop off USB bus under load
probable_causes.test: Connect MIDI device directly to computer USB port bypassing all hubs; test for dropouts

probable_causes.cause: DAW buffer size too small causing MIDI processing overrun during CPU-heavy sessions
probable_causes.test: Increase DAW audio buffer to 256 or 512 samples and observe if MIDI dropouts correlate with CPU spikes

steps.instruction: Replace USB cable with a high-quality cable under 1.5 m and connect directly to computer USB 2.0 or 3.0 port
steps.verification: Zero MIDI dropout events during 10-minute recording session in DAW MIDI monitor

steps.instruction: In DAW settings increase audio buffer size to 256 samples minimum to reduce CPU pressure on MIDI processing
steps.verification: CPU meter stays below 70% during recording; no note dropouts in MIDI event list

steps.instruction: If using DIN-5 MIDI connection, inspect DIN connectors for bent pins and reseat firmly
steps.verification: Physical connection secure; MIDI signal continuous with no glitches

problems.name: Speaker Distortion in Amplifier
problems.description: Guitar or bass amplifier speaker produces unwanted distortion, crackling or rattling unrelated to gain settings.
visual_symptoms: Crackling or rattling sound independent of gain or volume setting
Distortion audible even at low volume with clean channel selected
Speaker cone visibly torn or with hole in centre
Sound cuts in and out when speaker cabinet is moved

probable_causes.cause: Speaker cone torn or voice coil damaged from over-powering or physical impact
probable_causes.test: Gently press speaker cone inward by hand — grinding or scratching indicates damaged voice coil

probable_causes.cause: Loose cabinet panel, speaker mounting screw or grille vibrating sympathetically
probable_causes.test: Apply hand pressure to various cabinet panels while playing to identify which stops the rattle

probable_causes.cause: Dirty or oxidised speaker output jack causing intermittent signal interruption
probable_causes.test: Wiggle speaker cable at both ends while playing; crackle correlating with movement confirms oxidised jack

steps.instruction: Tighten all speaker mounting bolts and cabinet panel screws; add foam gasket behind grille frame if vibrating
steps.verification: Rattle eliminated when pressing panel; no sympathetic vibration at any volume

steps.instruction: Clean output and speaker jacks with contact cleaner spray; plug and unplug cable 10 times to polish contacts
steps.verification: Crackling not reproducible by cable wiggling after cleaning

steps.instruction: If speaker cone is torn, replace speaker with matching impedance and wattage rating
steps.verification: Replacement speaker matches original ohm rating; clean undistorted tone across all frequencies

problems.name: Bow Hair Snap
problems.description: Bow hair strands break or the entire ribbon snaps, preventing the bow from producing sound on string instruments.
visual_symptoms: Loose strands of bow hair hanging from ribbon
Gap or complete separation in bow hair ribbon
Rosin not adhering evenly due to uneven remaining hair
Tone thin and scratchy from reduced hair contact

probable_causes.cause: Over-tightening bow hair beyond recommended tension causing fatigue failure
probable_causes.test: At correct tension, bow stick should be approximately 1 cm from hair at midpoint — excess gap indicates over-tightening

probable_causes.cause: Natural wear from extended play sessions depleting horse hair fibres
probable_causes.test: Count approximate remaining hairs — below 120 hairs for violin bow indicates significant wear

probable_causes.cause: Rosin buildup creating brittle residue that snaps hair under dynamic stress
probable_causes.test: Inspect hair surface for thick white rosin crust — excessive buildup makes hair brittle

steps.instruction: If fewer than 50% of hairs remain, take bow to a luthier for professional rehairing
steps.verification: Rehaired bow has full even ribbon of approximately 150–180 hairs for violin; tone full and responsive

steps.instruction: Always loosen bow tension to 1–2 turns after each playing session
steps.verification: Bow hair shows slight slack when tightening screw is fully released

steps.instruction: Clean excess rosin from hair with a dry cloth wipe after each session to prevent brittle buildup
steps.verification: Hair surface shows thin even rosin coating without thick white crust

problems.name: Piano Sustain Pedal Failure
problems.description: Piano or keyboard sustain pedal does not engage, engages permanently or produces no effect on note duration.
visual_symptoms: All notes sustain indefinitely regardless of pedal state
Pedal pressed but notes cut off normally as if pedal not engaged
Pedal feels loose or has no mechanical resistance
Pedal cable connector visibly damaged at instrument jack

probable_causes.cause: Pedal polarity inverted — keyboard configured for opposite pedal polarity
probable_causes.test: Power cycle keyboard with pedal NOT connected; reconnect pedal — if notes now sustain correctly, polarity was inverted

probable_causes.cause: TRS jack connector on pedal cable damaged causing short or open circuit
probable_causes.test: Measure continuity across pedal jack with multimeter: open when up, closed when pressed should match keyboard expectation

probable_causes.cause: Acoustic piano sustain mechanism spring broken or damper felts worn
probable_causes.test: Lift lid and observe damper felt pads while pressing pedal — dampers not lifting indicate broken spring or worn mechanism

steps.instruction: Power cycle the keyboard with sustain pedal disconnected to allow automatic polarity detection
steps.verification: Keyboard powers on without pedal connected; reconnect pedal — notes sustain only when pedal is pressed

steps.instruction: Check keyboard settings menu for sustain pedal polarity setting and toggle it if automatic detection is unavailable
steps.verification: Pedal behaviour correct: notes hold when pressed, release when lifted

steps.instruction: If pedal cable connector is damaged, replace cable or solder new TRS jack onto existing cable
steps.verification: Multimeter shows open when pedal up and closed when pedal down; instrument responds correctly

problems.name: Microphone Feedback Loop
problems.description: Microphone picks up its own amplified output through speakers, creating a loud continuous feedback tone.
visual_symptoms: High-pitched squeal or howl from speaker system
Feedback tone changes pitch when microphone is moved
Gain reduction immediately stops feedback
Specific frequency ringing visible as spike on EQ analyser

probable_causes.cause: Microphone positioned in front of or too close to loudspeaker
probable_causes.test: Move microphone behind speaker line — if feedback stops, positioning was the cause

probable_causes.cause: Excessive gain before feedback (GBF) margin exhausted by high input gain or monitor volume
probable_causes.test: Reduce monitor send level by 6 dB; if feedback stops, monitor level was driving system past GBF limit

probable_causes.cause: Room resonance at specific frequency amplifying feedback at lower gain settings
probable_causes.test: Apply parametric EQ notch at feedback frequency; if consistent feedback frequency, room mode is factor

steps.instruction: Immediately reduce channel fader or master output until feedback stops, then reposition microphone behind speaker line
steps.verification: Feedback stops; microphone positioned at least 1 m behind nearest speaker and not in speaker projection path

steps.instruction: Use a feedback eliminator or apply narrow parametric EQ notches at problem frequencies identified during soundcheck
steps.verification: System gain raised 6 dB above previous feedback point without triggering feedback

steps.instruction: Switch microphone to cardioid polar pattern and aim null of pattern toward monitors
steps.verification: Monitor level increased 3 dB without feedback; microphone null confirmed pointed at monitor

problems.name: Keyboard Sustain Stuck On
problems.description: Keyboard sustain is permanently active, causing all notes to continue sounding regardless of pedal position.
visual_symptoms: All notes continue ringing after keys are released with pedal up
MIDI monitor shows no sustain-off message (CC64 value 0) when pedal released
Sustain indicator in DAW stuck in on position
Powering off and on resolves issue temporarily

probable_causes.cause: MIDI sustain controller message (CC64) stuck-on due to data corruption or dropout
probable_causes.test: Send MIDI all-notes-off and all-controllers-reset from DAW; check if sustain clears

probable_causes.cause: Pedal switch contact dirty or sticking in closed position
probable_causes.test: Unplug pedal cable entirely — if sustain immediately clears, pedal contact or cable is stuck closed

probable_causes.cause: Keyboard sustain button or pedal input PCB trace short circuit
probable_causes.test: Test pedal on a different keyboard instrument; if sustain still sticks, fault is in pedal; if not, fault is in keyboard

steps.instruction: In DAW, send MIDI Reset or All Controllers Off message to keyboard on affected channel
steps.verification: Sustain clears immediately; notes stop ringing without pedal pressed

steps.instruction: Unplug sustain pedal, clean TRS jack contacts with contact cleaner and test pedal continuity with multimeter
steps.verification: Pedal shows open circuit when released; closed circuit only when pressed

steps.instruction: Replace sustain pedal if contact is permanently closed or internal switch mechanism is faulty
steps.verification: New pedal shows correct open/closed behaviour; keyboard sustain responds correctly in all modes

problems.name: Condenser Mic Phantom Power Issue
problems.description: Condenser microphone fails to operate, produces no signal or produces distorted signal due to phantom power problems.
visual_symptoms: Microphone produces no audio output with 48V phantom enabled
Microphone hums loudly or produces clipping at low signal levels
Phantom power LED on interface lights but microphone unresponsive
Signal level drops dramatically compared to other microphones on same interface

probable_causes.cause: Phantom power not enabled on interface channel or enabled after microphone was already connected
probable_causes.test: Verify 48V phantom button is lit on channel; toggle off and on with microphone connected

probable_causes.cause: XLR cable with broken pin 2 or 3 preventing phantom voltage from reaching microphone capsule
probable_causes.test: Swap XLR cable with a known-good cable and test signal; if signal restored, cable has broken conductor

probable_causes.cause: Microphone internal capacitor or phantom circuit damaged from hot-plugging without phantom off
probable_causes.test: Test microphone on a second interface with fresh phantom supply; persistent failure confirms microphone damage

steps.instruction: Disable phantom power, connect microphone with XLR cable, then enable phantom power — always connect before enabling phantom
steps.verification: Phantom LED active on channel; microphone produces clear signal on voice test

steps.instruction: Replace XLR cable with a known-good cable, ensuring it is wired pin 1-1, 2-2, 3-3 with no shield breaks
steps.verification: Microphone signal level restored to expected level; no hum from cable

steps.instruction: If microphone still fails on known-good cable and interface, send to manufacturer for internal capsule or electronics repair
steps.verification: Microphone signal verified against specification output level after service
